title: Reject `--cpus` above host CPU count on podman machine init and set
body:
Fixes: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28322

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

For more detailed information, please review our contributing guidelines:
https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests
-->

#### Checklist

Ensure you have completed the following checklist for your pull request to be reviewed:
<!-- Use [x] to mark as done, or click the checkbox after opening PR -->

- [ ] Certify you wrote the patch or otherwise have the right to pass it on as an open-source patch by signing all
commits. (`git commit -s`). (If needed, use `git commit -s --amend`).  The author email must match
the sign-off email address. See [CONTRIBUTING.md](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#sign-your-prs)
for more information.
- [ ] Referenced issues using `Fixes: #00000` in commit message (if applicable)
- [ ] [Tests](https://github.com/containers/podman/tree/main/test#readme) have been added/updated (or no tests are needed)
- [ ] [Documentation](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/README.md) has been updated (or no documentation changes are needed)
- [ ] All commits pass `make validatepr` (format/lint checks)
- [ ] [Release note](https://github.com/kubernetes/community/blob/master/contributors/guide/release-notes.md) entered in the section below (or `None` if no user-facing changes)

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
Write `None` if there are no user-facing changes, otherwise enter your release note below.
Include "action required" if users need to take action when upgrading.
-->

```release-note
The `podman machine init` and `podman machine set` now reject `--cpus` values larger than the number of logical CPUs on the host, with a clear error instead of accepting an invalid value.
```

comments.body:
I know that libkrun has its own CPU limit, which could be smaller than the host machine's CPU count. I am not sure how to check that without hardcoding the value. Is there a dynamic way to check this so our code doesn't break if libkrun changes in the future? Alternatively, we can provide a better error message to users and call it a day. 

WDYT? @jakecorrenti

> I know that libkrun has its own CPU limit, which could be smaller than the host machine's CPU count. I am not sure how to check that without hardcoding the value. Is there a dynamic way to check this so our code doesn't break if libkrun changes in the future? Alternatively, we can provide a better error message to users and call it a day.
> 
> WDYT? @jakecorrenti

Functionally speaking, if the user were to provide more vCPUs than the limit of 16 it won't fail, the CPU count will stay at 16.

We set `CONFIG_NR_CPUS` in the guest kernel so there's currently no way to determine the max CPU count unless you're already in the guest AFAIK. However, we might be able to change this so that the kernel cmdline in libkrun uses `nr_cpus=n` and have an API that can get the value we set for `n`.

WDYT of this approach @slp? I know this would only work for variants where we don't set `CONFIG_CMDLINE_OVERRIDE=y` in the kernel, but this is only an issue on TEE kernels. Additionally, this wouldn't apply in scenarios where the user provides their own kernel, but that could just be noted in the API documentation.

> > I know that libkrun has its own CPU limit, which could be smaller than the host machine's CPU count. I am not sure how to check that without hardcoding the value. Is there a dynamic way to check this so our code doesn't break if libkrun changes in the future? Alternatively, we can provide a better error message to users and call it a day.
> > WDYT? @jakecorrenti
> 
> Functionally speaking, if the user were to provide more vCPUs than the limit of 16 it won't fail, the CPU count will stay at 16.

This limitation is in `libkrunfw` (the library bundling the custom kernel), not in `libkrun` itself. Since podman-machine doesn't make use of `libkrunfw`, it's not affected by this limitation. It can set up as many vCPUs as the hypervisor allows to configure.

LGTM
restarting what appears to be flakey tests
